questions.question: Define an array and state any two advantages of using arrays.
keyPoints: Array is a linear data structure storing elements of same type in contiguous memory
Two advantages: O(1) random access by index
Contiguous memory allocation enables cache-friendly access
Simple implementation and easy to use

questions.question: Differentiate between linear search and binary search. Under what conditions can binary search be applied?
keyPoints: Linear search checks each element sequentially - O(n)
Binary search divides search space in half each time - O(log n)
Binary search requires sorted array
Binary search requires random access (array)

questions.question: Explain the process of inserting an element at the beginning of an array. What is the time complexity?
keyPoints: Insertion at beginning requires shifting all elements one position right
Start from the last element and shift to the right
Insert new element at index 0
Time complexity is O(n) because all n elements need to be shifted

questions.question: Calculate the address of element A[3][2] in a 2D array stored in row-major order with base address 1000, 4 rows, 5 columns, and element size 2 bytes.
keyPoints: Row-major formula: Address = Base + ((i × n) + j) × size
The address is 1034

questions.question: What are the limitations of arrays as data structures?
keyPoints: Fixed size - cannot change at runtime (for static arrays)
Expensive insertions and deletions at arbitrary positions - O(n)
Wastes memory if allocated more than needed
Not suitable when number of elements is unknown

questions.question: Explain the difference between row-major and column-major order for 2D arrays.
keyPoints: Row-major: elements of each row stored contiguously, then next row
Column-major: elements of each column stored contiguously, then next column
C, C++, Java use row-major order
Fortran uses column-major order

questions.question: Write an algorithm to find the second largest element in an array.
keyPoints: Traverse the array once maintaining largest and second largest
Initialize largest and second largest with first two elements (compare and assign properly)
For each remaining element, update largest and second largest
Time complexity: O(n), Space complexity: O(1)

questions.question: Write a C program to insert an element at a specific position in a 1D array. Handle the case when the array is full or the position is invalid.
keyPoints: Declare array with maximum size
Check if array is full before insertion
Check if position is valid (between 0 and n)
Shift elements from the insertion position to the right
Insert the new element and increment count
Display the updated array

questions.question: Explain the binary search algorithm with a suitable example. Write the algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Binary search works on sorted arrays only
Initialize low = 0, high = n-1
Find mid = (low + high) / 2
Compare mid element with target
If equal, found; if less, search right half; if more, search left half
Repeat until found or low > high
Time complexity: O(log n), Space complexity: O(1) for iterative, O(log n) for recursive

questions.question: Write a Python program to delete an element from an array. Explain the different cases of deletion and their time complexities.
keyPoints: Deletion at beginning: O(n) - shift all elements left
Deletion at end: O(1) - no shifting required
Deletion at middle: O(n) - shift elements to fill the gap
Handle case when element not found
Handle underflow (empty array) case

questions.question: Explain how a 2D array is represented in memory. Derive the formula for calculating the address of an element in both row-major and column-major order.
keyPoints: 2D arrays are stored as 1D arrays in memory
Row-major: elements stored row by row
Column-major: elements stored column by column
Row-major address: B + ((i × n) + j) × S
Column-major address: B + ((j × m) + i) × S
Where B is base, i,j are indices, n,m are columns/rows, S is size
